input_path: data\papers\Heithoff et al. - A Digital Shadow for Accurate Robot Motion Control Integrating Data with Friction Models.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The physical system under study is the MABI MAX 100 six-axis serial industrial robot used in complex manufacturing tasks such as machining. Its key components include motors, gearboxes, and encoders, with high path repeatability and flexible rotary joints enabling precise operations in dynamic industrial environments.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) methodologies including RFLP, OOSEM, SPES, and SMArDT manage system complexity across abstraction levels (requirements, functional, logical, and technical layers). Architecture description languages (ADLs) such as ACME, AADL, MontiArc, and SysML Internal Block Diagrams (IBDs) model components, interfaces, and connections, with MontiArc Automaton and SysML state machines for behavior description. Simulation tools like MathWorks Simulink, additional simulation methods, and general-purpose programming languages (C++, Java) are employed for detailed behavior modeling and implementation.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin system utilizes Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) methodologies including RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) and SysML for structural modeling. Architecture Description Languages (ADLs) such as AADL, ACME, and MontiArc define components, interfaces, interconnections, and composition. Behavioral models employ MontiArc Automaton and SysML v1.7 state machines, while analytical simulations use MathWorks Simulink, general-purpose code (C++, Java), and other simulation tools. Data management integrates heterogeneous sources across abstraction levels (requirements, functional, logical, technical) and stakeholder perspectives.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Physical acting components include a permanent magnet synchronous motor with gear transmission, controlled by a Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl controller. Safety constraints specify maximum working range and maximum achievable speed, with precise addressing mechanisms for actuator control.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: Sensing infrastructure comprises two position encoders per drive, with a motor encoder directly on the drive shaft and a secondary encoder positioned behind the gearbox. Data is collected as time series from all sensors, providing high-frequency measurements for real-time monitoring.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: Model fidelity is ensured through sensor arrangements that minimize positional uncertainty. Validation is conducted by analyzing deviations between simulated and measured data, with systematic error correction and uncertainty quantification processes to maintain data integrity.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Time series sensor data (e.g., input current and output angle) streamed via MQTT protocol. Data aggregation and abstraction are performed for specific purposes as defined in the digital shadow reference model.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: MQTT communication protocol used for streaming sensor data. No details on network topology, bandwidth, latency, reliability, redundancy, or security protocols are provided in the document.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The twinning process follows a structured methodology including Problem identification, Asset and model analysis, Use or creation of calculations or models, and Data identification steps, as specified in Section II.C of the document. This approach ensures systematic development by first defining the problem scope, analyzing the physical asset and its models, developing necessary computational or simulation models, and identifying relevant data sources for integration.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The system provides decision support through specific analytics for detecting friction loss, resulting in improved robot control accuracy. User interaction occurs via dashboard visualization, enabling real-time monitoring and actionable insights for operational decisions.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The Digital Twin system complies with industry standards including SysML (including IBDs), AADL, ACME, and MontiArc as architecture description languages used in Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) for system modeling.

conclude in Sec. VII.


II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extraction is highly accurate and specific, correctly identifying the MABI MAX 100 six-axis robot, its use in complex manufacturing tasks like machining, and its key components (motors, gearboxes, encoders) and characteristics (flexible rotary joints, high path repeatability) as detailed in the text. Quotes: 'serial robot MABI MAX 100 with six axes', 'suitable for complex manufacturing tasks such as machining', 'flexible rotary joints, high path repeatability', 'motor... gear transmission... cycloidal gear... position encoders'.

The extracted characteristic states 'Not in Document'. The source documents focus on the creation and implementation of a 'Digital Shadow' rather than a full 'Digital Twin'. While the paper mentions that Digital Shadows serve as a baseline for Digital Twins and mentions 'visualization in a dashboard', it does not explicitly list or describe specific services provided by a Digital Twin (such as monitoring, optimization, or control) in the detailed manner required by the characteristic description. The document primarily discusses the shadow's role in data integration and friction model identification. Since the characteristic specifically asks for *Digital Twin* services and the document mainly addresses the *Digital Shadow* (stating 'A Digital Twin (DT) can provide such active components' but implementing the shadow), the specific DT services are not the focus of the text. Thus, the extraction's claim that this is 'Not in Document' is reasonable in the context of the paper's specific scope.

The extracted characteristic provides a detailed list of tools, methodologies, and languages, which is fully supported by Section II.A of the source text. The text explicitly lists 'RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics), OOSEM, SPES, or SMArDT' as methodologies for handling complexity. It lists 'ACME, AADL, MontiArc... or SysML IBDs' as architecture description languages. Finally, it mentions 'MathWorks Simulink... or code in general-purpose languages like C++ or Java' for behavior modeling. The extraction accurately reflects the technical content provided in the background section.
